title: Monomer

infobox.key: [Authority control databases](/wiki/Help:Authority_control "Help:Authority control"): National [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q178827#identifiers "Edit this at Wikidata")

infobox.value: ([/ˈmɒnəmər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*MON-ə-mər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); *[mono-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mono- "wikt:mono-")*, "one" + *[-mer](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-mer "wikt:-mer")*, "part") is a [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") that can [react](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") together with other monomer molecules to form a larger [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") chain or two- or three-dimensional network in a process called [polymerization](/wiki/Polymerization "Polymerization").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-clayden_organic-3)
A molecule which can undergo polymerization, thereby contributing constitutional units to the essential structure of a [macromolecule](/wiki/Macromolecule "Macromolecule").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Young, R. J. (1987) *Introduction to Polymers*, Chapman & Hall [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-412-22170-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-412-22170-5 "Special:BookSources/0-412-22170-5")
[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry, *et al.* (2000) *IUPAC Gold Book*, **Polymerization**](http://goldbook.iupac.org/P04740.html)

[Clayden, Jonathan](/wiki/Jonathan_Clayden "Jonathan Clayden"); Greeves, Nick; [Warren, Stuart](/wiki/Stuart_Warren "Stuart Warren"); [Wothers, Peter](/wiki/Peter_Wothers "Peter Wothers") (2001). *Organic Chemistry* (1st ed.). Oxford University Press. pp. 1450–1466. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-19-850346-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-850346-0 "Special:BookSources/978-0-19-850346-0").

Jenkins, A. D.; Kratochvíl, P.; Stepto, R. F. T.; Suter, U. W. (1996). ["Glossary of basic terms in polymer science (IUPAC Recommendations 1996)"](http://pac.iupac.org/publications/pac/pdf/1996/pdf/6812x2287.html). *[Pure and Applied Chemistry](/wiki/Pure_and_Applied_Chemistry "Pure and Applied Chemistry")*. **68** (12): 2287–2311. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1351/pac199668122287](https://doi.org/10.1351%2Fpac199668122287).

D. Margerison; G. C. East; J. E. Spice (1967). *An Introduction to Polymer Chemistry*. Pergamon Press. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-08-011891-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-08-011891-8 "Special:BookSources/978-0-08-011891-8").

Ebuengan, Kaye. ["Biomolecules: Classification and structural properties of carbohydrates"](https://www.academia.edu/5082714). *Academia.edu*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Molecule which reacts with other monomers to form a polymer

A **monomer** ([/ˈmɒnəmər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*MON-ə-mər*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); *[mono-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mono- "wikt:mono-")*, "one" + *[-mer](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-mer "wikt:-mer")*, "part") is a [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") that can [react](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") together with other monomer molecules to form a larger [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") chain or two- or three-dimensional network in a process called [polymerization](/wiki/Polymerization "Polymerization").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-clayden_organic-3)

[IUPAC](/wiki/International_Union_of_Pure_and_Applied_Chemistry "International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry") definition

> **Monomer molecule**: A molecule which can undergo polymerization, thereby contributing constitutional units to the essential structure of a [macromolecule](/wiki/Macromolecule "Macromolecule").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

[Chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") classifies monomers by type, and two broad classes based on the type of [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") they form.

By type:

* natural vs synthetic, e.g. [glycine](/wiki/Glycine "Glycine") vs [caprolactam](/wiki/Caprolactam "Caprolactam"), respectively
* polar vs nonpolar, e.g. [vinyl acetate](/wiki/Vinyl_acetate "Vinyl acetate") vs [ethylene](/wiki/Ethylene "Ethylene"), respectively
* cyclic vs linear, e.g. [ethylene oxide](/wiki/Ethylene_oxide "Ethylene oxide") vs [ethylene glycol](/wiki/Ethylene_glycol "Ethylene glycol"), respectively

By type of polymer they form:

* those that participate in [condensation polymerization](/wiki/Condensation_polymerization "Condensation polymerization")
* those that participate in [addition polymerization](/wiki/Addition_polymerization "Addition polymerization")

Differing [stoichiometry](/wiki/Stoichiometry "Stoichiometry")[[5]](#cite_note-5) causes each class to create its respective form of polymer.

:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Condensation_polymerization_diacid_diamine.svg/960px-Condensation_polymerization_diacid_diamine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Condensation_polymerization_diacid_diamine.svg)

    This nylon is formed by [condensation polymerization](/wiki/Condensation_polymerization "Condensation polymerization") of two monomers, yielding water

The [polymerization](/wiki/Polymerization "Polymerization") of one kind of monomer gives a [homopolymer](/wiki/Polymer#Monomers_and_repeat_units "Polymer"). Many polymers are [copolymers](/wiki/Copolymers "Copolymers"), meaning that they are derived from two different monomers. In the case of condensation polymerizations, the ratio of [comonomers](/wiki/Comonomer "Comonomer") is usually 1:1. For example, the formation of many nylons requires equal amounts of a [dicarboxylic acid](/wiki/Dicarboxylic_acid "Dicarboxylic acid") and [diamine](/wiki/Diamine "Diamine"). In the case of addition polymerizations, the comonomer content is often only a few percent. For example, small amounts of 1-octene monomer are copolymerized with ethylene to give specialized polyethylene.

## Synthetic monomers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Synthetic monomers")]

* [Ethylene](/wiki/Ethylene "Ethylene") gas (H2C=CH2) is the monomer for [polyethylene](/wiki/Polyethylene "Polyethylene").
* Other modified ethylene derivatives include:
  + [tetrafluoroethylene](/wiki/Tetrafluoroethylene "Tetrafluoroethylene") (F2C=CF2) which leads to [Teflon](/wiki/Teflon "Teflon")
  + [vinyl chloride](/wiki/Vinyl_chloride "Vinyl chloride") (H2C=CHCl) which leads to [PVC](/wiki/PVC "PVC")
  + [styrene](/wiki/Styrene "Styrene") (C6H5CH=CH2) which leads to [polystyrene](/wiki/Polystyrene "Polystyrene")
* [Epoxide](/wiki/Epoxide "Epoxide") monomers may be cross linked with themselves, or with the addition of a co-reactant, to form [epoxy](/wiki/Epoxy "Epoxy")
* [BPA](/wiki/Bisphenol_A "Bisphenol A") is the monomer precursor for [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate")
* [Terephthalic acid](/wiki/Terephthalic_acid "Terephthalic acid") is a comonomer that, with [ethylene glycol](/wiki/Ethylene_glycol "Ethylene glycol"), forms [polyethylene terephthalate](/wiki/Polyethylene_terephthalate "Polyethylene terephthalate").
* [Dimethylsilicon dichloride](/wiki/Dimethylsilicon_dichloride "Dimethylsilicon dichloride") is a monomer that, upon [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis"), gives [polydimethylsiloxane](/wiki/Polydimethylsiloxane "Polydimethylsiloxane").
* [Ethyl methacrylate](/wiki/Ethyl_methacrylate "Ethyl methacrylate") is an [acrylic monomer](/wiki/Acrylate_polymer#Monomers "Acrylate polymer") that, when combined with an acrylic polymer, catalyzes and forms an acrylate plastic used to create artificial nail extensions

## Biopolymers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Biopolymers")]

The term "monomeric [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein")" may also be used to describe one of the proteins making up a [multiprotein complex](/wiki/Multiprotein_complex "Multiprotein complex").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Natural monomers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Natural monomers")]

Some of the main [biopolymers](/wiki/Biopolymer "Biopolymer") are listed below:

### Amino acids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Amino acids")]

For *proteins*, the monomers are [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid"). Polymerization occurs at [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome"). Usually about 20 types of amino acid monomers are used to produce proteins. Hence proteins are not homopolymers.

### Nucleotides

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Nucleotides")]

For polynucleic acids ([DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA")/[RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA")), the monomers are [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide"), each of which is made of a [pentose](/wiki/Pentose "Pentose") sugar, a nitrogenous base and a phosphate group. Nucleotide monomers are found in the [cell nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"). Four types of nucleotide monomers are precursors to DNA and four different nucleotide monomers are precursors to RNA.

### Glucose and related sugars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Glucose and related sugars")]

For carbohydrates, the monomers are monosaccharides. The most abundant natural monomer is [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose"), which is linked by [glycosidic bonds](/wiki/Glycosidic_bonds "Glycosidic bonds") into the polymers [cellulose](/wiki/Cellulose "Cellulose"), [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch"), and [glycogen](/wiki/Glycogen "Glycogen").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Isoprene

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monomer&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Isoprene")]

[Isoprene](/wiki/Isoprene "Isoprene") is a natural monomer that polymerizes to form a [natural rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber"), most often *cis-*1,4-polyisoprene, but also *trans-*1,4-polymer. [Synthetic rubbers](/wiki/Synthetic_rubber "Synthetic rubber") are often based on [butadiene](/wiki/Butadiene "Butadiene"), which is structurally related to isoprene.
